skillName: Multiply and divide integers and decimals by powers of 10
misconceptionSummary: Students add zeros to the end of decimal numbers when multiplying by 10, giving wrong answers.

visuals.ruleText: Multiplying by 10 shifts every digit one place to the LEFT — the decimal point stays fixed

steps.narration: When you multiply by ten, every digit moves one place to the left in the place value table. The decimal point does not move — the digits do. Three point four seven becomes thirty-four point seven.
Each extra power of ten is an extra shift. Multiplying by one hundred shifts two places left. Two point five becomes two hundred and fifty.
Division by a power of ten shifts digits to the right. Four hundred and seventy divided by one hundred shifts two places right — the answer is four point seven.

visuals.label: × 10ⁿ → shift n left.  ÷ 10ⁿ → shift n right.

steps.narration: Multiply — shift left. Divide — shift right. Count the zeros in the power of ten to find how many places to shift.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: 3.47 × 10 = 3.470 (adding a zero at the end). The digits shift left — 3.47 × 10 = 34.7. Never add a zero to a decimal when multiplying by 10.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Multiplying by 10 moves digits left — it does not add a zero. 3.47 × 10 = 34.7. Adding a zero gives 3.470, which equals 3.47 — unchanged.
